raw_payload.author: Hakihiko
raw_payload.content:
"The Bluff" is one of those films that doesn't aim high but still manages to do its job fairly well. It's not profound, nor particularly ambitious, but it knows how to keep the audience engaged from start to finish.

The plot is straightforward and doesn't try to be overly complex. There aren't many surprises, and the themes remain surface-level, yet the narrative flows smoothly enough to maintain interest. The pacing helps a lot; it moves at a steady rhythm that avoids long dull moments.

Performances are solid. The cast carries the material competently, giving just enough credibility to make the story work. Nothing groundbreaking, but nothing distracting either.

Overall, "The Bluff" is quite entertaining for what it is. It may not leave a lasting impression or offer much depth, but it delivers a watchable and enjoyable experience.

raw_payload.author: SAMTHEBESTEST
raw_payload.content:
The Bluff (2026) Movie Review -

"They are coming to get Bloody Mary. Bloody is what they're gonna get," says Mary, aka Priyanka Chopra Jonas, announcing just how badass she intends to be - and yes, it is fun for a while. But the golden rule of pirate flicks feels broken here. A pirate film overloaded with gunshots loses a chunk of its charm; it's the swords, the humour, the parlays, and the witty conversations that truly complete this genre. Over the years, we have seen classics like Captain Blood (1935), The Princess and the Pirate (1944), Pirates of the Caribbean (2003), and the iconic silent swashbuckler featuring Douglas Fairbanks. Those days seem long gone, so perhaps it isn't entirely fair to blame The Bluff for being modern and somewhat mediocre. In today's landscape, mediocrity is common currency - so yes, The Bluff is entertaining, but very much a routine watch.

The Bluff follows Mary (Priyanka Chopra Jonas), a former pirate who has reformed into a family woman with a husband, sister, and son. Her past soon catches up when Captain Connor (Karl Urban) - the man she once stabbed, betrayed, and robbed - returns seeking revenge, his gold, and perhaps even his woman. With her family now in the line of fire and her husband taken hostage, Mary is forced back into survival mode on the island as Connor and his gang of merciless killers close in. The big question remains: can she handle it her way? Find out in the film.

The film wastes no time setting up the plot but leaves several moments feeling immaturely incomplete. Take the opening scene - Mary senses someone in her sister's room but neither checks properly nor catches the intruder, which feels oddly convenient. The action sequences rely heavily on familiar clichés: villains who won't shoot when they clearly can, pausing instead to deliver dramatic dialogue and conveniently allowing the heroin to strike back. One unintentionally funny moment involves armed men failing to kill a crocodile-a sequence that had me questioning their IQ. The husband's instant forgiveness and sudden burst of bravery also feel formulaic. Yet, despite these flaws, the film maintains a brisk pace and, importantly, doesn't put you to sleep like Citadel occasionally did. It may feel dated, but it remains a reasonably engaging swashbuckling entertainer - even if guns do most of the talking instead of swords and hand-to-hand combat.

Priyanka Chopra Jonas continues her search for a truly memorable Hollywood role, and the wait continues with The Bluff. She certainly gets to kick plenty of assses in what is an aggressively physical part, and she performs competently, but nothing here feels particularly iconic. Karl Urban carries the aura of a deadly villain initially, only to fall into predictable territory by the end. Ismael Cruz Córdova has limited screen time but makes a decent impression. Safia Oakley-Green's pretty face works visually, though her performance could have used more depth. Vedanten Naidoo, meanwhile, looks more like a daughter than a son in certain moments. Temuera Morrison and the rest of the supporting cast are serviceable.

Technically, the film is fairly adequate. The cinematography is pleasant without being striking, the background score does its job but rarely elevates the drama, and the editing maintains a decent pace throughout. The coastal locations and island sets are visually appealing, thanks largely to the production design. A handful of dialogues do carry whistle-worthy value. Director Frank E. Flowers may have delivered a somewhat dated actioner and arguably missed the core spirit of a true pirate movie, but he never lets the film completely sink - and that itself is a small win. Prime Video often backs projects that feel more suited to home viewing than theatrical spectacle, so expecting grand big-screen magic might be unrealistic. Still, for a one-time small-screen watch, The Bluff offers modest, time-pass entertainment - even if the fun remains limited.

RATING - 5/10*

raw_payload.author: Ashiiish
raw_payload.content:
The Bluff sells you pirates, revenge and survival in the Caribbean. What it really gives you is Priyanka Chopra fighting like her life depends on it... inside a film that doesn't fully know what it wants to be.

Let's get this straight. Priyanka Chopra is the backbone of this movie. She doesn't phone it in. She bleeds into the role. Her action sequences feel raw and physical. No unnecessary slow motion hero shots. No exaggerated stunts. Just grounded, brutal choreography. Easily the strongest thing here.

The action staging at times reminded me of John Wick. Close combat. Smart use of props. Tight spaces. You can feel the impact. The background score by Henry Jackman adds punch and urgency where needed. Cinematography by Greg Baldi gives us some genuinely good looking frames of the Caribbean. Karl Urban does fine as the antagonist. The supporting cast holds up.

But here's the problem.

The film starts with promise. Then somewhere in the second act, it just starts drifting. Instead of building tension through story, it keeps stacking action scenes. After a while, it becomes predictable. You've seen this structure before. It feels safe and formula driven.

And for something promoted as a pirate film, the sea barely matters. There's an exciting sea battle early on, and then the ocean almost disappears. It shifts into a home invasion style thriller more than a pirate saga.

Some action scenes are drowned in dark lighting and CGI, which makes it harder to follow what's actually happening. And those classic moments where characters pause to deliver monologues to the person they're about to kill. That kind of writing kills the intensity instantly.

It's streaming on Amazon Prime Video. If you're in the mood for solid action and want to see Priyanka in full combat mode, go for it. Just walk in with low expectations.

raw_payload.author: lissa_luvs_movies
raw_payload.content:
I must admit I do not fully understand the bad reviews. Let's start off by stating the obvious, it's a dang pirate movie!!! How original can they make it, I mean a pirate, is a pirate, is a pirate; they are known for specific things. This movie does those things pretty darn well. If you started watching this movie hoping to find a new Jack Sparrow, you know nothing about what a pirate was.

I get it the movie became predictable, but it did throw a few unexpected twists at you. Priyanka did a brilliant job, she carried the movie, but she was the focus of the entire movie so carrying the movie rested heavily on her shoulders. The other actors did great too. I watched because I'm a fan of Karl Urban, I thought he did a good job for what his character was.

I thought the setting was gorgeous, the action was entertaining and done well. I never once thought to myself of that's a stunt double for sure, the fight scenes were seamless. The acting wasn't forced by anyone. For these reasons I gave 6 stars. It's not as terrible as some reviewers try to convince you it is. Watch it for it is, a pirate movie (not Jack Sparrow's version of a pirate) packed with action, fights, and blood. Now, let me say this I love Jack Sparrow, I have nothing against dear Jack, but Jack was not a "real pirate", for lack of a better term, he was brilliant in his own right so you cannot watch other pirate movies and try to compare them to him.

So I suggest, take a chance, give it a go. You might be surprised. Let's now address the question, would I watch this movie again? My answer would be yes. Its a well done average movie. Nothing special, nothing outstanding, just good, simple entertainment. On to the next.

raw_payload.author: Kamandi73
raw_payload.content:
Priyanka Jonas puts a lot of energy into her performance, but the story is very formulaic. There are no surprises. It is by the numbers.

Karl Urban is one of my favorite actors, but he is just strolling through the scenes, like he does on The Boys. Not much effort, nothing special from him. I guess since the story was nothing special, Urban just showed up and read his lines.

Boba Fett, Temuera Morrison, puts a lot more into his character. Fett has passion, and makes a powerful impression. Unfortunately, Fett has less screen time than Urban.

The cgi effects resemble a Sci-fy channel movie. In a movie where all the killing is mostly from knives and swords, it would have been nice to see some old-fashioned blood that was not cgi.

raw_payload.author: daisukereds
raw_payload.content:
A family's home is invaded by violent men seeking riches. Very much a 2002's "Panic Room" with a Pirate costume in Halloween, or a more dangerous "Home Alone" type of premise.. where the female lead needs to survive and protect their family.

And while I feel the core premise could be easily switched to a different genre or context and would work just fine.. the use of Pirates and the setting is appealing enough (Karl Urban as a pirate Captain? Sign me up!).

Conceptually, I really like the idea of a dying breed seeking freedom through betrayal and a reward.. where there's also an element of love, but one's survival is more important. The execution is good, visually pretty and entertaining. Suffice to say their choice of words were not the best, as most interactions and one-liners could have been much better and have more depth.

I would have also liked to be surprised, for it to have more layers. As it stands, it is perfectly okay.

raw_payload.author: SoumikBanerjee1996
raw_payload.content:
"Tonight they came looking for Bloody Mary and Bloody is what they're gonna get"

There, that was the real 'Bluff', hidden cleverly in the lines uttered. I should've seen it coming from miles away, but my fondness for both Priyanka and Karl clouded my judgment.

It's as predictable and cliched as you'd expect from films of this type, yet I held onto a flicker of hope primarily because the action and the gritty, contrasty color grading in the trailer led me to believe that it will at least provide some mindless entertainment, if nothing else.

But boy! Was I wrong!

Dialogues were horrendous, characters felt disposable, and the second-rate CGI really dulled whatever potential there might have been.

Even the action scenes which I was genuinely excited about, quickly became a drag, falling into a tiring loop of repetition, mainly because of unimaginative choreography and of course over reliance on second rate CGI projection!

As for the performances, I'll say this: both Karl and Priyanka delivered the most stereotypical versions of themselves (Priyanka channeling her character from Citadel and Karl straight from The Boys) and it just didn't mesh well.

raw_payload.author: ktrawick
raw_payload.content:
So it was no Raiders or Pirates of the Caribbean. But it was okay for an afternoon with nothing going on. Lots of dead guys, killer Caimans and explosions galore. The acting was meh, but the setting was beautiful.

Some of the reviews are confusing, especially one that cited something about phone calls. Not sure what movie that person watched. But all in all, it was a decent made for TV flick.

raw_payload.author: masonsaul
raw_payload.content:
The Bluff is a thoroughly predictable action movie that has just enough action sequences and wastes no time getting things going, even though it does intermittently slow down. The blunt dialogue hinders this by deliberately over-explaining everything in a clunky fashion to account for any potential second screen viewing but it has a solid well worn premise, game cast and a 102 minute run time that ensures the film doesn't overstay its welcome.

Priyanka Chopra Jonas gives a really good leading performance with the necessary stoic determination and anguish when she has to return to a life she never wanted to see again. Despite his shaky accent, Karl Urban is still a good villain who maintains a restrained presence even as he becomes increasingly motivated by revenge at any cost. Safia Oakley-Green's performance is undone by how frustrating her character's actions are.

Co-writer & director Frank E. Flowers delivers the best and bloodiest fight scene early on and the rest of the film doesn't entirely live up to the promise of subsequent scenes being at a similar quality but everything afterwards continues to be nicely staged and pretty brutal. Greg Baldi's cinematography is always coherent without remotely rivalling his previous work and Henry Jackman's lively score definitely matches the setting.

raw_payload.author: FiftyTwo_52
raw_payload.content:
Went in with low expectations, typical Prime direct-to-streaming fare, and those expectations were pretty much met. Very average watch. 5.5/10.

The landscapes are genuinely beautiful, but the script and direction absolutely let this down. Priyanka gives it her full commitment, throwing herself into the physical demands completely, but that's not nearly enough.

The real failure is that we don't feel for her character at all, which is purely shoddy direction. The headline story and theme are actually decent - there's a good movie buried somewhere here - but the execution fumbles it badly. Karl Urban looks genuinely bored, sleepwalking through his role without any real engagement. You can tell he's cashing a paycheck.

The action is competent when it arrives, but you're never emotionally invested enough to care. For a story about a mother fighting to protect her family, the lack of emotional connection is fatal.

Watchable but completely forgettable.

raw_payload.author: AshleyO-699
raw_payload.content:
Honestly this was not the movie I thought I was going to end up watching, I had seen no trailer or no particular advertisement, so I was going in blind only knowing it was something to do with pirates.

But it really isn't, it's just another generic action movie in costume. I love action movies, but since John Wick brought a new energy to the genre almost everything that has followed minus a few gems (Extraction) has almost felt like a rip off. Like I said I was expecting a pirate themed movie but it never quite feels like one. Supposingly this is set sometime within the 19th century, I didn't feel that, the movie never took the energy and the themes that previous pirate themed movies had so ultimately it just became another action movie.

And some of the action is fine, occasionally looks good, creative with some of the scenes, but ain't that just most movie's nowadays, I just didn't see anything new. What little story it has was pretty lame, I didn't relate or feel anything for any of the characters, and by the end I realised the filmmakers and writers just struggled with any sort of concept. Which ultimately led them saying let's hire a female lead and dress them in costume because realistically that's the only thing that's different than a lot of other movies out there.

But it's not enough. I think some people will enjoy this, especially the younger generation that are brought up on these type of action movies, I just wish these movies cared as much about the story and characters as they did finding new ways to blow someone up.

The acting is fine, nothing special, outside of Karl Urban I didn't really know anyone, and for a while now Urban has been making these straight to streaming type movies so the quality of the movie by the end was no suprise to me. The only light suprise I did have was I felt Priyanka Chopra (Ercell) did better than expected, but my expectations of her performance were extremely low. But she held her own and looked to be really hands on especially with the stunt work and fighting scenes so I respect that.

But in conclusion, it's not something I'll watch again, it did little for me in terms of entertainment, the premise was pretty weak and ultimately in a very crowded action genre this will be forgotten about quickly.

raw_payload.author: ruidanielbarrossss
raw_payload.content:
I liked this one. It's basically Die Hard on a island, and that setup never really gets old. Chopra plays Ercell, a woman trying to live a quiet life in the Caribbean. Things fall apart when pirates invade the island because of [...spoilers]. Karl Urban plays the captain leading the raid, and he has a serious grudge against her.

Chopra is good here. She looks believable in the action scenes and handles the sword fights and hand to hand combat well. The fights and action overall are solid.

Urban brings his usual intensity. His character could have used more depth, but he still works as the villain and is especially effective in the action scenes, which are the real focus of the movie.

This pirate flick does not try to reinvent anything. It knows what it is and keeps things moving. If you want simple, straightforward action for a lazy evening, this gets the job done.

raw_payload.author: cgrc-254-535005
raw_payload.content:
I can't be the only person who is sick and tired of the boss lady trope.

You know the type. She can easily dispose of men twice her size and three times as strong by a punch or a roundhouse kick. It's so tedious. Yes I know it's a movie, but fight scenes should be grounded in reality. Any one of the men she goes up against would easily kick her backside.

There are many more issues with this flick. Some of the accents, in particular Karl Urban's.

Some of the acting, in particular everyone.

The CGI is a bit ropey to say the least.

Kids might well love this movie but adults should give it a body swerve.

raw_payload.author: Nigel_
raw_payload.content:
They went to the trouble of getting good actors but I had some many issues. The general style is a parody of a Disney movie.

Its pirates, but everything is shinny.

The camera man was on something its shaking all over the place and bopping up and down even when nothing is happening.

Insanely close shots so much they cut off the top of the actors heads.

Zero background in focus, I imagine they went cheap on the stages and it was filmed in a cupboard.

I almost lost it in the first half when the camera jumps to a person while they are talking then slowly moves up to their face and all for zero reason.

Karl Urban's accent I have no idea what it is supposed to be, he should have just kept his own.

Its pouring with rain and wind, they are standing on ship deck and they are perfectly dry.

I need to check the credits and see if the Cameraman ever filmed something or this is a style choice by the insane director.

But the insane framing and ridiculous zooming is in almost every shot.

Just checked its Greg Baldi who has never shot a film before.

I think all the people who made "creative" choices for the movie were way way out of their depth.

The story looks a bit ropy but to be fair the camera work took be so out if it I have no idea what the story was. Something about gold and stealing it.

I could see this been a cheap pilot for a series that got canceled because it was so bad but it cost to much to simply write off so they stuck it on streaming.

raw_payload.author: MovieKing1956
raw_payload.content:
Well shiver me timbers, what a pleasant surprise this big budget MGM/Netflix Pirate movie was - it's really decent and action packed from start to finish!

Underrated New Zealand actor Karl Urban takes top billing and is excellent as the merciless villain of the piece, as is his super feisty female nemesis, played by gorgeous former Miss World/Bollywood star Priyanka Chopra Jonas (someone sign her up as the next leading Bond girl!).

The storyline is straightforward enough. Urban is looking for his gold stash (natch) which has been stolen by his 'reformed' ex-partner in crime (Chopra Jonas) and is hidden somewhere on her secluded paradise Island home. From the moment Urban's pirates land the action (and bloodshed) is relentless. The locations are fantastic (especially the hideout caves beneath the Bluff), the pacing/editing is fast, the dialogue suitably short and snappy, the weaponary used is most interesting and numerous high octane fight scenes are well staged and executed.

Indeed the treasure hunt cat-and-mouse games continue apace to an exciting finale and satisfactory conclusion that doesn't disappoint. Honestly, there's not much wrong with this hidden gem - imo it puts the corny, kiddy-ish 'Pirates of the Carribean' franchise to shame.

So, if you like fast-paced actioners, blood-soaked fighting and pirate movies (who doesn't) i heartily reccommend this one, it's a real surprise. Now, just go watch it you scurvy dogssss ;-P.

raw_payload.author: rainoffireproductions
raw_payload.content: I'm dying here with these reviews. I supposed some of you think Pirates of the Caribbean is better than this film! LOL. I hadn't seen a descent pirate film in a very, very long time. It's action packed, it's bloody, and it's visually stunning. It's almost like John Wick meets Pirates!!! The story is excellent and the characters are very well fleshed out. This is a $50 million pirate production made for adults. Enjoy!!! We don't get too many of those! Most pirate films are kiddie crap!

raw_payload.author: michelecarmignani
raw_payload.content:
I really enjoyed The Bluff. It's a solid action movie that is both fun and entertaining. Priyanka Chopra Jonas is excellent; she is charismatic and perfect for this role. The action scenes are very well-made and choreographed, featuring a good amount of gore that fans will appreciate.

I honestly don't understand the low ratings this movie is receiving. I wonder if it's because some people still have an issue with a strong, smart, and capable female lead. If you judge the movie for what it is-a great action piece-it's definitely a 7.5 or 8/10. It delivers exactly what it promises. Highly recommended!

raw_payload.author: hamadasari
raw_payload.content:
The Bluff surprised me in a good way. I expected a straightforward pirate action movie, but it ended up feeling more personal than that. Beneath the sword fights and shootouts, it's really about a woman trying to outrun who she used to be - and realizing that some ghosts don't stay buried.

Priyanka Chopra Jonas carries the film with a performance that feels both strong and vulnerable. You can believe her as someone dangerous, but also as a mother who's scared of losing everything. The action scenes are intense without being chaotic, and the setting gives the movie a raw, old-school adventure vibe.

It's not groundbreaking, and parts of the story are predictable, but it has heart. I cared about the characters, and that's what made it work for me.

raw_payload.author: UnitedStories
raw_payload.content:
While the narrative of The Bluff sails through well-charted waters, the film's execution-anchored by a career-defining physical performance from Priyanka Chopra Jonas-gives it a sharp, modern edge.

Priyanka Chopra Jonas doesn't just play Ercell (the legendary "Bloody Mary"); she inhabits her with a raw, muscular conviction. Known for her "professional hazards" on set, Priyanka reportedly tackled the majority of her own stunts, and it shows. She strikes an impressive balance between the "glitz" of a global star and the "grit" of a woman who has spent her life at the edge of a blade.

The true "hidden character" of the film is its masterful sound design. The audio landscape is dense and immersive-from the metallic ring of a cutlass to the unsettling silence of the Caribbean mangroves. It's a technical achievement that begs for a large-format theater.

The film follows a classic "nemesis from the past" blueprint. By gender-swapping the lead to a mother protecting her family, the movie had a golden opportunity to raise the stakes. However, while the action is top-tier, the emotional tissue remains a bit thin.

Highlights to Watch For The "Home Alone" Trap Sequence: Ercell's use of the island's terrain to set MacGyver-like traps for the invading pirates is a creative highlight.

Cinematography: The contrast between the lush, sun-drenched Cayman Islands and the R-rated "splatter" violence creates a unique visual identity.

Pacing: At a lean 101 minutes, the film never overstays its welcome, moving from one kinetic set piece to the next.

Final Verdict: The Bluff is a decent watch that succeeds primarily as a high-octane showcase for Priyanka Chopra Jonas's evolution into a legitimate action heavyweight. It's a polished, brutal, and atmospheric thriller that makes for a perfect Friday night stream.

raw_payload.author: grahamchilds-21768
raw_payload.content:
Awful!

It's starts with a "historical statement " saying the Caribbean pirates were dying out in 1846.

That's a hundred years too late.

Karl Urban's Irish accent is grating.

None ever existed.

The pirate quarter master has a pronounced New Zealand accent which is slightly out of place since Europeans didn't colonise that country until a hundred years after the pirates died out.

0/10.

raw_payload.author: rehmankhilji
raw_payload.content:
Firstly, keep your expectations low, not too high and you may manage to watch it till the end. It certainly a movie made for the small screen and at several points fails to align with its own theme. For instance, why would pirates be covering their head and faces in black veils? It simply made no sense and necessary

As for the cast, Karl Urban seems stuck somewhere between being a hero and a villain and at times even between a pirate and a cowboy. The character lacked clarity and unfortunately, the performance did not elevate it either.

Priyanka Chopra gives the role her full effort, but the overall impact remains underwhelming. Her accent also fluctuates noticeably in the movie.

In conclusion, it is strictly a one time watch and that too only when you have nothing better to see. Once again, approach it with minimal expectations.

raw_payload.author: stevelivesey-37183
raw_payload.content:
The first half hour is pretty good. Lots of action and character development but it can't be maintained and I began to loose interest.

The is a well made film. The cinematography, the production sets, the cast are all good. But the problem is that the characters of Chopra and Urban are portrayed as superheroes, killing multiple people with ease. Not realistic.

This seems like a rejected script from a Pirates of the Caribbean movie that didn't get made.

The cliches just keep coming toward the end as did the lack of swashbuckling and humour.

It's ok if you are having POTC withdrawal.

raw_payload.author: gaever-60669
raw_payload.content: Priyanka Chopra Jonas remains, along with Jennifer Garner, the most reliable action heroine. The Bluff is an old-fashioned violent film - likely more the way it was with pirates in 1846. Plenty of swordplay, old-time muskets, explosions, light on any real plot development. You get what you get and it's pretty good.

raw_payload.author: henry8-3
raw_payload.content:
Ercell Bonner (Priyanka Chopra) lives a peaceful life on the tropical island of Cayman Brac with her son and daughter-in -law waiting for her husband to return. She has a secret though, for she was the dreaded pirate Bloody Mary who stole gold from her partner Connor (Karl Urban) and keeps it buried on the island. He has now returned to Cayman Brac to get the gold and his revenge.

Pirate movies with the exception of Pirates of the Caribbean are something of a rarity these days. In large part this is because you can only go so far with the Treasure Island ooo arr stuff. What you have here though is a largely enjoyable bit of swash buckling thanks to a good pairing of Urban and Chopra who are both perfectly capable of tearing folk asunder. They do this on a regular basis and the makers don't pull their punches when it comes to viscera and keeping it tough - PG viewing this is not. It's not entirely original of course with lots of nasty pirates, buried gold etc but it's still a fun ride.

raw_payload.author: TheMovieSearch
raw_payload.content:
The Bluff sails in promising high seas adventure and swagger, the kind of swashbuckling chaos that makes pirate tales irresistible. I love a good pirate movie, the grit, the myth, the danger humming under every plank of the ship. This one, unfortunately, never quite finds its sea legs. Instead of roaring like a cannon blast, it drifts like a ship caught in windless waters.

Priyanka Chopra Jonas is undeniably talented. When she steps into law enforcement roles or grounded action dramas, she commands the screen with authority and conviction. Here, as the infamous Bloody Mary, she feels miscast. The performance is not terrible, but it feels disconnected from her strengths. The ferocity that should define a feared pirate legend never fully materializes. Rather than dominating the frame, she often seems to be searching for the character instead of embodying her.

The film itself feels like a story nested inside another story, yet neither layer receives the focus it deserves. The narrative tries to juggle myth, revenge, and survival but never commits to a clear throughline. Bloody Mary's legend is talked about more than it is shown. For someone supposedly hunted and feared, she rarely projects the kind of commanding presence that justifies a bounty on her head.

When you think of formidable female action icons like Kimberly from Mighty Morphin Power Rangers, or warriors such as Xena and Buffy Summers, their strength radiated consistently. Toughness was not situational. In The Bluff, the character often feels reactive rather than powerful, surviving by circumstance instead of strategy or dominance.

By the final act, the sense of missed opportunity becomes clear. The atmosphere, the mythology, and the lead talent all hint at something grander. Yet the film never fully commits to building a truly fearsome pirate saga. What remains is a production with flashes of potential that never quite connects into the epic it aims to be.

raw_payload.author: Bryyycers
raw_payload.content:
Had no expectations going in, as I hadn't even heard of the project until I stumbled across a couple different trailers earlier in the day. What I found wasn't just another swashbuckling adventure, but a homage to a bygone era of storytelling.

At its core, The Bluff is a familiar story, but its charm lies entirely in its execution. The film plays out almost as if you were watching it live on a theatrical stage. From the blocking of the actors to the very rigging of the theatrics. It heavily channels the spirit of early 1900s pirate plays and silent films, think J. M. Barrie's original 1904 staging of Peter Pan or Douglas Fairbanks' The Black Pirate (1926). You can practically feel the wooden floorboards beneath the actors' boots and see the painted canvas of the horizon. To be clear, this stage-like approach takes absolutely nothing away from the movie, it's the films strength. Do not expect the cgi spectacles of Captain Jack or the adventure of Doctor Jones.

Ultimately, The Bluff is a modern throwback. It takes today's filmmaking techniques and uses them to recreate the feeling of early twentieth-century stage and screen. It is a love letter to the origins of the adventure genre, delivering a classic story told with old-school theatrical flair.

A refreshing, fun, and theatrical voyage well worth taking, but do not expect anything new here.

raw_payload.author: okpilak
raw_payload.content: Sort of an old fashioned swashbuckling pirate film, and fun to watch. Captain Conner overtakes a smaller boat, and on it finds a gold bar. He recognizes it, and forces T. H Bodden, the vessel's captain, to tell where the rest of the gold may be. They don't land gently on the island, and go to the home of Ercell. She proves that she is not some gentle fisherman's wife with kids, and manages to dispatch the pirates sent to the house. So what is the story about Ercell, and why is Captain Conner after her and the gold? It is a simple enough story, and fun to watch and enjoy. Probably goes better with some popcorn.

raw_payload.author: rogerio_soares
raw_payload.content: All of us who read Robert Louis Stevenson as children carry within us the imagery of pirate stories and buried treasures waiting to be discovered. Well, this film, while not a treasure, is still, more or less, a pirate story; that is, it has pirates but no story. It's an amalgam of action movie clichés, where a woman now plays the role of an Aussie Rambo. In short, you watch it because you want to know how this kind of nonsense might end. And it has an ending that anyone could easily predict, thanks to the absolute lack of imagination of the screenwriters...

The writing of the movie is b grade at best it comes out as your Typical girl boss movie absolute DRIBBLE.... Alas if your into that type of garbage you will probably enjoy it.

raw_payload.author: SnowAngeI
raw_payload.content:
FRANK E. FLOWERS' "The Bluff" arrives on Prime Video dressed in the grit and gore of 19th-century Caribbean piracy, but beneath the salt-crusted exterior lies a screenplay that never earns the violence it so eagerly dispenses. Set in 1846 on the limestone bluffs of Cayman Brac, the film follows ERCELL "Bloody Mary" BODDEN - a former pirate attempting domestic reinvention - as her past resurfaces in the form of CAPTAIN CONNOR, a vengeful ex-captain hunting stolen gold.

PRIYANKA CHOPRA JONES is the film's lone structural anchor, and she commits fully; her Ercell is a persuasive femme fatale whose physicality in the combat sequences sells the brutality that Flowers clearly wants to foreground. The choreography, to its credit, strips away the theatrical swashbuckling Hollywood has long romanticized, opting instead for a messier, more visceral register of pirate violence - though this authenticity is routinely undercut by opponents who freeze at conspicuously convenient intervals, granting Ercell the breathing room that realism would deny her.

KARL URBAN, meanwhile, does what he can with Captain Connor, but the character is introduced with such brevity and so little psychological texture that he registers as a stock heavy rather than a credible foil; there is no ideological friction between hunter and hunted, no counter-argument to Ercell's worldview, just a snarling presence with a grudge and - in a lapse that quietly eviscerates the production's period credibility - a double-action revolver not patented until 1851, five years after the film's own stated timeline.

The larger disappointment, however, is architectural: events proceed in a dogged "and then" cadence, one set piece chasing the next without the connective tissue of consequence shaping subsequent decisions, leaving characters who are already thinly sketched with no room to behave as though their choices carry weight. Character-driven beats surface occasionally but land with minimal emotional gravity, because the film has never paused long enough to let us feel the interior lives fueling them.

TEMUERA MORRISON delivers a surprisingly muted performance, and the supporting ensemble functions primarily as an exposition pipeline for Ercell's backstory, though SAFIA OAKLEY-GREEN briefly transcends this utility with a flash of genuine facial range that the rest of the script never affords her.

Flowers lands a handful of striking compositions in the island exteriors, and Ercell's combat armor stands apart in an otherwise unremarkable wardrobe, but these are ornamental victories in a film whose direction never reconciles its appetite for kinetic spectacle with the patience that earned catharsis demands.

The conclusion arrives not with the weight of a reckoning but with the deflation of an obligation met, and what lingers afterward is the absence of a story rather than the presence of one - a film that swings its blade relentlessly yet never finds anything beneath the surface worth cutting into.

For viewers content with second-screen pirate fare, The Bluff may scratch a modest itch; for anyone seeking storytelling that respects its own premise, the title is the most honest thing about it.

raw_payload.author: emwee609
raw_payload.content:
I don't even know where to start. I had seen the trailer and expected a decent pirate action film. After all, the cast includes some big names: Priyanka Chopra Jonas, Karl Urban, and Temuera Morrison-best known as Boba Fett. But wow, was I disappointed. It's rare for me to feel this way, but I seriously considered stopping the film halfway through.

The movie actually starts quite well, but then it goes downhill rather quickly. Overall, the acting is weak, and most other elements are mediocre at best. The only performers who seem to put real effort into their roles are Temuera Morrison and Priyanka Chopra Jonas, the latter playing the protagonist, Bloody Mary-a ruthless female pirate who has left the pirate life behind to start a family and live peacefully on an island.

The side characters, especially Bloody Mary's family members, are among the most annoying I've seen on screen in a long time. They gave me the same kind of frustration as Sandro Radiani in Extraction 2, Filip Inaros in The Expanse, and Luke Malik in Invasion. If you know those characters, you know exactly the level of irritation I'm talking about. As with those cases, I don't blame the actors here-the problem lies entirely in the writing. These poorly written characters alone cost the film at least two stars in my rating.

Another major issue is the dialogue. It's often silly, with characters trying too hard to speak in an imagined old-English manner-at least early on. Later, it almost feels like they forgot that approach altogether. The action scenes are generally okay, but an important battle near the end takes place in such a dark cave environment that it's often difficult to see what's happening or who's doing what.

That's especially unfortunate, because the film's strongest elements are the bloody action, the cave setting reminiscent of The Goonies, the booby traps, and, of course, the hidden pirate treasure. In short, the movie had a lot of potential, but it never really delivers on it. I sincerely hope there won't be a sequel-ever.

raw_payload.author: sadidsifat
raw_payload.content:
Welcome to slop era of low effort garbage. So here is a very well written review this movie deserves.

The kind of movie that feels assembled from trend forecasts and streaming data rather than a singular creative vision. Marketed as a high-stakes psychological thriller, it follows a troubled strategist drawn into a dangerous game of deception after a mysterious figure from his past resurfaces. On paper, it promises tension, twists, and moral ambiguity. In execution, it mostly delivers mood lighting and dramatic pauses.

The film opens strong, with slick cinematography and a pulsing electronic score that suggests something bold and contemporary. There's a moody cityscape, cryptic phone calls, and a protagonist who communicates inner turmoil through prolonged staring and minimal dialogue. Unfortunately, atmosphere is doing most of the heavy lifting. The script leans heavily on exposition disguised as "clever" banter, and the supposed mind games rarely rise above predictable double-crosses.

Characters feel more like archetypes than people: the morally conflicted lead, the enigmatic femme fatale, the smug antagonist who speaks in rehearsed monologues about truth and power. Their motivations are hinted at but rarely explored, as if the movie assumes complexity simply by withholding information. When the inevitable third-act twist arrives, it feels less shocking than perfunctory-a box checked on a thriller checklist.

To be fair, Bluff looks expensive. The production design is sleek, the editing crisp, and the cast delivers competent performances despite thin material. There are flashes of what could have been a sharper, more character-driven story buried beneath the gloss.

Ultimately, Bluff isn't a disaster; it's just hollow. It gestures toward depth without committing to it, mistaking ambiguity for sophistication. By the time the credits roll, you may admire the polish, but you're unlikely to remember much about the game it tried so hard to play.

raw_payload.author: TechI-1
raw_payload.content:
The Bluff: A Confusing Mess That Misses the Mark

The Bluff is easily one of the most disappointing films I've watched in a long time. It deserves a solid 0 out of 10. From start to finish, it felt like a confused mess with no clear direction, no engaging story, and no sense of purpose. I genuinely struggled to understand what the movie was trying to be. Was it supposed to be a pirate film? A modern action drama? Some kind of hybrid? Whatever it was aiming for, it completely missed the mark.

The plot is painfully weak and all over the place. There's no real structure, no meaningful buildup, and no payoff. Scenes feel randomly thrown together, as if the filmmakers hoped style would cover up the lack of substance. Instead, it just creates frustration. Important moments carry no emotional weight because the story never gives you a reason to care. By the time anything remotely significant happens, you're too confused or disconnected to be invested.

The acting doesn't help either. Performances feel flat and unconvincing, with characters lacking depth or personality. It's hard to tell whether the actors were miscast or simply working with poor material, but the result is the same - nothing feels authentic. Dialogue often sounds forced or unnatural, making it even harder to stay engaged.

The tone is another major issue. The movie seems unsure of its identity, constantly shifting in ways that don't feel intentional or creative - just inconsistent. Instead of blending genres in an interesting way, it ends up feeling chaotic and unfocused. There's no clear atmosphere to hold everything together.

Overall, The Bluff is confusing, poorly executed, and ultimately forgettable. It's a film that promises intrigue but delivers frustration. I wouldn't recommend it to anyone, and I honestly wish I could get my time back.

raw_payload.author: dkent213
raw_payload.content:
The Bluff is proof that you can pour millions into a production and still end up with something that feels like it was fished out of a bargain bin at a petrol station. It's dark, it's intense, it's pirate-themed... and it's about as thrilling as watching paint dry on a sinking ship.

The premise had potential. An ex-pirate wrestling with his past, shadows closing in, morally grey decisions, storms both literal and emotional - on paper, that's solid. You can practically smell the salt air and betrayal. But somewhere between concept and execution, the whole thing just collapsed into a moody, joyless slog.

Let's start with the cast. I'm all for discovering new talent. Genuinely. But when a film asks you to invest two hours of your life in a grim, heavy narrative, you need something to hold onto. A familiar face. A bit of charisma. A spark. Instead, we get a lineup of actors who feel like they were assembled from a "Serious Expressions Monthly" catalogue. No presence. No magnetism. Just blank stares and gravelly whispers.

And that's the real crime here: boredom. This film isn't offensively bad in a hilarious way. It's not chaotic fun. It's not "so bad it's good." It's just... flat. Endless brooding. Endless darkness. Endless scenes where people glare at each other like they're auditioning for "Most Miserable Human 2026."

The tone is relentlessly grim. Every scene feels dipped in grey sludge. Pirate films - even darker ones - should still have tension, danger, maybe even a twisted sense of adventure. Instead, The Bluff feels like it actively resents the idea of being entertaining. There's no swash, no buckle. Just moody pacing and dialogue that sounds like it was written by someone who thinks slow equals deep.

And here's the bigger frustration: the money. You can see the budget on screen. The production design is polished. The cinematography tries its absolute hardest. The sets look expensive. The lighting screams "prestige." But what's the point of throwing cash at a project if the heart of it - the performances and the script - are this lifeless?

It's like buying a £5,000 sound system to play elevator music.

There's also this growing pattern where big streaming platforms keep backing projects that feel algorithm-designed rather than passion-driven. Dark tone? Check. Anti-hero? Check. Moody soundtrack? Check. Emotional trauma flashbacks? Triple check. But personality? Nowhere to be found.

By the halfway mark, I wasn't intrigued - I was clock-watching. By the final act, I just wanted it to end. And when it finally did, I didn't feel moved, shaken, or impressed. I felt relieved.

That's not what cinema is supposed to do.

The Bluff could have been something. The bones were there. The idea wasn't terrible. But without compelling performances or even one recognisable anchor to pull viewers in, it just drifts - slowly - into cinematic oblivion.

Final verdict: glossy, expensive, aggressively dull. A pirate film with no treasure and no soul.

raw_payload.author: rossjayp
raw_payload.content:
As just a popcorn flick it is ok. I mean if you are scrolling Tik Tok while watching it and only paying attention to the fight scenes and don't think about our care about the the story too much, it is probably good. Not to nitpick too much, but jeez, can we have a little more pirating in a pirate movie? Other than the flashbacks and the opening scene there was ZERO time actually on a pirate ship. It was more like a really gruesome Goonies where the protagonist kills countless pirates in a house, on a river, in the forest, and of course in a pirate themed cave of wonders.

It has a great cast and each actor did a great job with the content they had to work with. Therein lies the problem, outside the action, the story itself was atrocious.

We are supposed to care about the lead (Chopra) who somehow wronged Urban's character. You only hear about her backstory through some very brief flashbacks. You never get a chance to actually care much about her. Her family was captured by pirates (Urban's) and rather than kill her, he trained her as a pirate. She eventually captains her own ship and then betrays Urban to settle down and start a family. The writer and director assume that the audience will just take her side of this story since she was only on the boat because she her family were indentured servants.

However, none that was Urban's characters fault. He was a Privateer, paid by the English to attack enemy ships and to enforce their trade routes.

He chose to save Chopra's character. Once she became a captain on her own, theoretically she could have just left without stealing from Urban's character. Instead, she betrayed the one person who showed her any kindness and gave her a chance. It just does not make any sense.

The throwaway line from Chopra that "he was colonizer for the East India Trading company" is supposed to simply cast Urban's character as the obvious villain in the story (it doesn't work). In fact it backfires. You actually feel more empathy for Urban's character. Here is a woman who he was like a daughter to him and she betrayed him. He follows her to the end of the world hoping to save her from a dead or alive bounty from the "three kingdoms".

He sacrifices all his wealth, his entire crew, and in the end his life for her. She stole his money and ran off with her lover to start a family, and in the end killed him for it.

This story would have been much better as a revenge story. They could have spent a little more time building up Chopra's character. Rather than short flashbacks about her time as a pirate, actually show it. Spend 30 minutes getting to know her as a person. Show her struggles growing up as the only female pirate and eventually becoming so ruthless that she captains her own ship. Create a story where Urban's character becomes jealous of her success and betrays her. She escapes, but is thought to be dead, she meets a man and settles down. Then Urban's character finds out she is still alive, and attempts to finish what he started. Have him chase her down and kill her family. That makes her come out of retirement to seek revenge. She was the bigger person, was betrayed, tried to leave that life behind to start a family, and he was the one that came back and made her (reluctantly) kill him to end it all. I would totally watch that movie, plus 3 or 4 sequels.

The supporting cast has enough to do to make the film work, but none of the supporting characters feel like they have much depth.

The pacing is also a problem. The movie starts off strong with an actively exciting open water sequence, but then it becomes a generic revenge/action/thriller. With something that's being promoted as a pirate movie you would think the water would stay a large part of the film. However, it becomes strangely irrelevant for the biggest part of the movie.

It is definitely watchable. There is plenty of action that keeps the movie moving, but ultimately, the movie is pretty forgettable and feels like a missed opportunity.

raw_payload.author: drael64
raw_payload.content:
Overall, this movie is entertaining and compelling. It paints a deadpan-serious, pirates-on-land kind of tale, with good acting, strong set pieces, and characters you can actually invest in. First and foremost, it's an action movie.

So I should get this out of the way: an action movie with a female lead can very easily strain plausibility because of the strength differential. This movie doesn't handle that perfectly. Atomic Blonde is a pretty good example on the fantastical end of making this work (moments of exhaustion, lots of reliance on weapons, moments of real danger). This movie does tend to gloss over the issue a little, but there are scenes that sell the need for weapons or tactics against stronger foes, which helps.

I feel like if this had been handled a bit more deliberately rather than trying to sell her purely as a super-badass by adding a few more moments of genuine desperation, the action would have felt more compelling. But as I said, it's not a total let down there, either. Much of the fighting isn't traditional swordplay or straight hand-to-hand brawling, and it remains a fun watch.

And combined with the solid characters, acting, and set pieces, I think they did pretty well.

It's also an original story (as opposed to a franchise or book adaption), which is genuinely rare these days. I'd probably rate it around a 6.5, but I'll bump it up to a 7 for that last point alone hoping Hollywood takes a few more risks like this, and tries to deliver stories that are not sequels, prequels, adaptions and reboots!

raw_payload.author: aparichitan

From the very first frame, this action film grabs you by the collar and refuses to let go. It delivers a relentless surge of adrenaline, blending explosive set pieces with razor-sharp pacing that never allows the momentum to dip. The fight sequences are choreographed with precision - every punch feels heavy, every chase scene pulses with urgency, and every explosion lights up the screen with cinematic intensity.

What makes the film stand out isn't just the spectacle, but the emotional stakes beneath it. The protagonist isn't invincible - there's grit, vulnerability, and a clear motivation driving every decision. That layer of humanity gives weight to the chaos, making the action feel earned rather than empty.

Visually, the cinematography enhances the experience with sweeping camera movements, dramatic lighting, and immersive close-quarters combat shots. The background score deserves special praise - it builds tension masterfully, rising at just the right moments to elevate the thrills.

raw_payload.author: saolivaresm
raw_payload.content:
Prime Video takes a gamble on a mix of pirates and action, directed by Frank E. Flowers and starring Priyanka Chopra and Karl Urban. The film starts strong thanks to an attractive premise, but soon settles into more conventional territory, missing the opportunity to delve deeper into its story and the universe it presents

In the 19th century, a woman named Ercell "Bloody Mary" Bodden (Priyanka Chopra) must confront her past when a group of pirates threatens the island where she lives with her family. Amidst shootouts, betrayals, and unexpected alliances, the story blends action and adventure in a swashbuckling world where survival depends on strength and cunning.

The film's greatest strength lies in its lead duo: Chopra convincingly embodies a survival heroine, while Urban solidly complements her presence. Both carry the narrative and prevent it from faltering, even when the script merely adheres to the conventions of a platform thriller. Flowers, for his part, demonstrates skill in the action sequences, ensuring that the fights and shootouts are both rhythmic and visually clear.

However, the film only scratches the surface. The potential of its pirate world and the tensions it raises are never fully explored, making it decent entertainment but lacking the depth that could have set it apart. It's functional cinema, designed for a good time, but it leaves you with the feeling that there was a more ambitious journey that was never undertaken.

It's an entertaining film that adequately fulfills its purpose. It feels like a platform movie, but it remains enjoyable once you get into what it's about. It had much more to explore in its story, and that's perhaps its biggest drawback, but for those looking for a carefree experience, it's a decent film that uses the pirate world to deliver some action and gunfights.
